add_finding.title: Lifecycle deprovisioning gap
add_finding.explanation: Terminated users take more than 3 days to be removed from Epic, and disabled AD accounts frequently remain active in Epic.
add_finding.business_impact: Terminated or disabled users may retain active access longer than expected, increasing access risk and audit exposure.

add_finding.title: Provisioning is almost entirely manual
add_finding.explanation: Less than 25% of users are provisioned through IAM and the primary method is fully manual.
add_finding.business_impact: Manual provisioning increases error rates, slows onboarding, and creates inconsistent access patterns across the organization.

add_finding.title: Provisioning automation does not extend to terminations
add_finding.explanation: IAM automation covers provisioning but termination deprovisioning lags, creating a gap in the lifecycle.
add_finding.business_impact: Incomplete lifecycle automation leaves a window of unnecessary access after employment ends.

add_finding.title: Excessive role and template sprawl
add_finding.explanation: Users average 7 or more templates and there is significant duplication or overlap across roles.
add_finding.business_impact: Role sprawl makes access reviews unreliable, increases the attack surface, and complicates any standardization effort.

add_finding.title: Exception-driven access model
add_finding.explanation: More than 60% of access is granted via exceptions and role structure is highly inconsistent.
add_finding.business_impact: An exception-driven model makes access reviews nearly impossible and erodes the value of any RBAC investment.

add_finding.title: RBAC standardization opportunity
add_finding.explanation: Users average 4–6 templates and a significant portion of access is exception-based.
add_finding.business_impact: Standardizing roles would reduce exception volume and improve the reliability of access reviews.

name: IAM coverage does not translate to lifecycle automation

add_finding.title: IAM coverage is not translating to lifecycle automation
add_finding.explanation: Most users are managed through IAM but lifecycle events (hire/transfer/terminate) are still handled manually.
add_finding.business_impact: IAM investment is not delivering its core value if lifecycle automation is not enabled.

add_finding.title: Provider onboarding remains a major manual dependency
add_finding.explanation: Despite broad IAM coverage, provider onboarding is largely manual.
add_finding.business_impact: Manual provider onboarding is a common bottleneck that delays access for clinical staff and increases administrative burden.

add_finding.title: IAM role enforcement is inconsistent
add_finding.explanation: IAM rarely enforces role-based provisioning consistently across the user population.
add_finding.business_impact: Inconsistent enforcement undermines the reliability of access controls and creates gaps that accumulate over time.

name: SSO and native auth posture inconsistency

add_finding.title: SSO adoption and native auth posture appear inconsistent
add_finding.explanation: High SSO adoption is reported alongside many users still using native Epic authentication.
add_finding.business_impact: Native auth bypasses centralized identity controls and may indicate SSO enforcement gaps.

add_finding.title: MFA is not enforced for Epic access
add_finding.explanation: Multi-factor authentication is not enforced for Epic access.
add_finding.business_impact: Without MFA, compromised credentials provide unrestricted access to the EHR environment.

add_finding.title: Elevated authentication risk
add_finding.explanation: A meaningful portion of users are authenticating natively through Epic while MFA enforcement is limited or absent.
add_finding.business_impact: This combination represents elevated credential risk across a significant portion of the user base.

add_finding.title: Access is being granted before training completion
add_finding.explanation: Users are receiving Epic access before completing required training, and untrained users with active access are not rare.
add_finding.business_impact: Untrained users with active access increase both compliance risk and the likelihood of improper data access.

add_finding.title: Training is poorly aligned to job roles
add_finding.explanation: Training assignments do not reflect the actual access and responsibilities of each role.
add_finding.business_impact: Misaligned training means compliance requirements may not be met and user competency cannot be assured.

add_finding.title: Access review cadence is insufficient
add_finding.explanation: Access reviews are conducted rarely or never.
add_finding.business_impact: Without regular access reviews, accumulation of unnecessary access goes undetected, increasing both risk and audit exposure.

add_finding.title: Audit and monitoring visibility is minimal
add_finding.explanation: No monitoring for anomalous access and minimal audit logging are both present.
add_finding.business_impact: The organization cannot detect inappropriate access or respond to security incidents effectively.

add_finding.title: Governance foundation is weak
add_finding.explanation: There is no clear ownership of Epic Security/IAM and no formal access governance structure.
add_finding.business_impact: Without governance, corrective actions are unlikely to be prioritized or sustained over time.

add_finding.title: No process for managing access changes
add_finding.explanation: Access changes are handled without any defined process.
add_finding.business_impact: Uncontrolled access changes create audit trail gaps and make it impossible to enforce least-privilege principles.

add_finding.title: HR, IAM, and Epic workflows are highly disconnected
add_finding.explanation: HR, IAM, and Epic operate in silos with no meaningful workflow alignment.
add_finding.business_impact: Disconnected workflows are the root cause of most lifecycle gaps — provisioning delays, termination lags, and access accumulation.

add_finding.title: Team is operating in reactive support mode
add_finding.explanation: The team is mostly reactive and rarely runs optimization initiatives.
add_finding.business_impact: A team without capacity for proactive work cannot sustain improvements or respond to growing organizational complexity.

add_finding.title: Operational maturity is at the firefighting stage
add_finding.explanation: The team describes its operational maturity as firefighting.
add_finding.business_impact: Teams in firefighting mode cannot prioritize strategic security improvements and are at risk of falling further behind.

add_finding.title: Maturity constraints appear structural, not isolated
add_finding.explanation: Both Governance and Provisioning scores are below 5, indicating that the gaps are not isolated to one area.
add_finding.business_impact: Structural gaps require coordinated improvement across people, process, and technology — point solutions alone will not close the maturity gap.

add_finding.title: IAM tooling appears ahead of governance maturity
add_finding.explanation: IAM scores are strong but governance is underdeveloped relative to the tooling investment.
add_finding.business_impact: Without governance to direct it, IAM tooling delivers less value and may create a false sense of security.

add_finding.title: Authentication controls are stronger than monitoring discipline
add_finding.explanation: Authentication posture is mature but audit and monitoring capabilities lag significantly.
add_finding.business_impact: Strong controls without monitoring means the organization cannot detect when those controls are bypassed or fail.

name: Team lacks capacity for role hygiene

add_finding.title: Team likely lacks capacity to sustain role hygiene
add_finding.explanation: Both Operational and RBAC scores are below 5, suggesting the team does not have the bandwidth to maintain role structure quality.
add_finding.business_impact: Without operational capacity, RBAC improvements will degrade over time even if initial cleanup work is done.
